Like he just said, we've worked on a lot of accounts together. It's been a lot of fun. All different types of acquisition models. So yeah, WFS group stands for wires from strangers. And the name sounds badass, right? But it actually refers to much more because to actually extract a wire from a stranger, we're talking about taking cold traffic and converting them into a trusted raving customer, right?"
[00:35] "And so, that's what I love about high ticket sales. And there's so many things that really have to happen to scale a sales operation. So, today I was talking to Jeremy and he's telling me, you know, when we're going to speak to this mastermind, he said, 'Dude, bring your best stuff. You know, bring some good stuff. I want to give them some really valuable stuff here.' And I know you guys are an elite group."

[01:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "High Leverage Tips for Scaling Integrity-Based Sales Ops"
NÚMEROS: 700+ slides mencionados
VISUAL: Título da apresentação principal, design profissional com foco em "integrity-based"
[01:20] "So, what I ended up doing for this, I made this presentation a handful of the most high leveraged tips for scaling integrity-based sales ops. And you guys might be thinking like, why did he feel the need to mention integrity base? And guys, I'll just tell you this. Having an elite sales operation that operates with integrity is so much harder than lying to people, right?"
[01:45] "And so I just want to be extremely clear here. I only talk about integrity based offers, integrity based sales operations. And all of the basics have to happen first before you can scale an offer. You have to have good intentions, right? You have to have strong fulfillment and like Jeremy and a couple other people mentioned today, even Ryan, you want to make sure what you're selling has a lot more value than the money that your customers exchange."

I used a bunch of outbound intelligence tools and I would make a lot of cold calls, lots of dials every single day."
[02:55] "And I'll tell you guys this, when I first started cold calling, I got good through getting hung up on hundreds of times. I've been hung up on more times than a lot of you guys in this room have made calls, right? And I don't say that to brag. I say it actually for the opposite. It was a very hard journey to learn this skill set of really high ticket sales, right?"
[03:15] "And so anyways, I started with outbound because I believe that that's the truest definition of generating money out of thin air. People that are intentionally reaching out or scheduling like Jeremy said earlier, that's why I love his new strategy. What do you call it?"
[03:30] "The hydra."
[03:32] "The hydra. I also love the fact that he brands like all his different strategies. It's funny, but great name. I love it because it compounds outbound strategies and tactics with inbound. I actually like it for a bunch of other reasons, but anyways, that's what I did."

That's the most basic minimum bare minimum thing that you could have, right?"

However, even though I scale sales operations, that's what I do best. I don't spend a lot of my time selling, right? It's all around the process stuff and everything else that I'm going to go over here today."
[07:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Main Variables We Measure"
NÚMEROS: 7 métricas principais
VISUAL: Gráfico circular ou dashboard mostrando: Lead Velocity, Lead Quality, Pipeline Velocity, Close Rate, Average Sales Price, Overall Volume, Profitability
[07:25] "So, when I was brainstorming, what am I going to talk about to really provide a lot of value to you guys here? How do I brainstorm the most high lever things to speak on? And so what I basically did is I just came up with the main variables that we're always measuring ourselves against as a sales operation, right?"
[07:45] "These are pretty much the main ones here. We're going to have lead velocity, right? Lots of leads. Lead quality, right? Most of us understand that that's a trade-off between the two, right? You could have more lead velocity by decreasing friction, but then you have lower lead quality. You could have higher lead quality by increasing friction and qualifications, but then you have less lead velocity, right? That's a balanced relationship."
[08:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pipeline Velocity"
NÚMEROS: Foco em redução de tempo do ciclo de vendas
VISUAL: Funil de vendas com setas indicando velocidade, cronômetro, comparação entre ciclos longos vs curtos
[08:20] "Then we're going to go over one of my favorite things called pipeline velocity, right? How many people in here have had a lot of good metrics on the front end of marketing and you close a lot of deals, but your sales cycles take forever, right? We've all seen that."
[08:35] "You want to collapse the sales cycle because here's the thing. I heard something a long time ago. I think a mentor said it to me at one point and he said, 'If you make a million in a month and a million in a year, you guys made the same amount of money. The only difference is the component of time.' So collapsing sales cycles is something that I spend a lot of time focusing on and implementing a lot of process to do so."
[09:00] "Obviously, we'll talk about close rate, but I'm probably going to talk about it in a different way that you guys usually hear. Then we'll talk about average sales price, overall volume, and profitability. Okay?"
[09:15] "Just because I was giving some context around it, I'm going to read them through once more. These are basically the main metrics for sales operations. You have lead velocity, lead quality, pipeline velocity, close rate, average sales price, overall volume, and profitability. In other words, you want to do all those things profitably or it was a waste."

[09:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Best Sales Strategy Is A Great Offer"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Texto em destaque, possivelmente com ícone de troféu ou estrela, design enfático
[10:00] "Okay, these are fairly straightforward, but it's also questions that I get all of the time and I think people overthink it and we have to go back to the basics. Okay, the best sales strategy is a great offer."
[10:15] "Okay, I'm going to read some of these and I'll give examples in between. Ryan, I love what you said. He was talking about the fact that the value exchange has to be so effortless and mindless in the lead or the prospect's mind. If you guys don't have a great offer, you could be the best closer in the world, it's going to feel like you're rollerblading uphill, right?"
[10:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Offer = Avatar's Problems + Desired Mechanism + Willingness to Pay"
NÚMEROS: 3 componentes principais
VISUAL: Equação visual ou diagrama de Venn mostrando a intersecção dos três elementos
[10:40] "Best sales strategy is a great offer. The best offer sells your avatar's problems where the desired and preferred mechanism is aligned with willingness to pay."
[10:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Travel Example: Current State → Desired State"
NÚMEROS: $50,000 para jato privado vs economia
VISUAL: Ilustração de viagem: ponto A para ilha (ponto B), comparação entre jato privado e voo econômico
[10:55] "Okay, here's an example of that. Let's say that you guys are taking a trip and you want to go to an island, right? Literally, I talk about current state and desired state. The sales process is just the bridge. Your current state in this literal physical example is you're here. The desired state is you want to go to an island."
[11:15] "Okay. Do you guys want to go on a private jet for free? Or would you rather take, you know, whatever, what do they even call economy? Or would you what would you rather do if they're free?"
[11:25] "You want to take a jet?"
[11:27] "Perfect. Okay, thank you. But a jet is 50 grand actually. Now, which one do you think you're going to do?"
[11:35] "Right in this example, the common sense tells us economy. So, in other words, we're solving their problem. We're getting them from point A to point B, right? Their desired mechanism is actually a jet, but the willingness to pay isn't aligned with that."
[11:50] "So, you see how the simplest thing makes it so your offer doesn't scale there, right? So, we're going to come back to this later, but the best offer, it solves their problem, but the desired mechanism is aligned with the willingness to pay. A lot of people misunderstand that gap. Okay?"
[12:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Price Point = Elasticity of Willingness to Pay vs Net Profit"
NÚMEROS: Fórmula de elasticidade
VISUAL: Gráfico de curva de demanda, balança equilibrando preço e volume, planilha de exemplo
[12:15] "The best price point is elasticity of willingness to pay versus net profit. Okay? People ask me all the time, what do we price our offer at? They overthink it constantly. Should I increase it? Should I decrease it? Such a dumb question. Just take it to a spreadsheet and it will tell you."
[12:35] "It's all going to be about elasticity, right? In price sensitivity, but it has to do with how much can I raise the willingness to pay where the frequency of buyers still nets me more profit. And you can only really calculate that with data and financial modeling. So, I'll come back to it later."
[12:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Price Sensitivity = Market Comparables + Differentiation"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo de diferença de preço: $25K vs $80K
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado de ofertas, gráfico de posicionamento de mercado
[13:00] "Price sensitivity will largely depend on market comparables and ability to differentiate. Who has any type of limited knowledge on real estate? Single family real estate, just very limited basic stuff. How do you value a house if you're going to price your house? What do they typically an agent will tell you how you base the price upon?"
[13:20] "Comparable sales."
[13:22] "Comparable sales. What have similar homes sold for recently in my area? Then they compare it. Same with the used car. You're going Kelly Blue Book, right? I'm selling this car. They have you put in all the information, the mileage, the year, the model, the make, whatever it is, right? Then they spit out a price that's comparable to that."
[13:45] "Okay? So, I want to tell you guys something. The market matters. If you guys have an offer and you better believe that people are using the same type of targeting that you are and if they have an offer at 25k and your guys' is 80k and you failed to differentiate, your offer will fail because the price sensitivity."
[14:05] "They're going to be too sensitive to the price because of market alternatives. So, it's largely dependent on the market comparables and the ability to differentiate. I cannot tell you guys how important that is."
[14:20] "I've consulted with so many clients and I dive into their stuff and I'm looking at their offer. And by the way, a lot of us are dealing with ROI based offers and info product or alternative education, however you describe it, doesn't matter. It's the same thing. And so I'm going to use a lot of those analogies today, even though this is true for all sales and lead generation."
[14:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real Example: $25K Offer Analysis"
NÚMEROS: $25K price point mencionado
VISUAL: Checklist de comparação de valor, análise competitiva
[14:50] "I get under the hood, right? They'll have an offer say, 'Hey, 25K.' And then they'll ask me this. I just met him. I always get asked this. This always comes to price. They say, 'Is that a good price?' I don't know. I don't know anything about your offer. Then I look into it and I tell them no, it's not."
[15:10] "But the reason I say no a lot of the times is because of comparables. If you just compare the value, the type of content, the quantity, all of these other things to what other people are selling it for, doesn't compare. Okay."
[15:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Platform/Funnels = Determined by Data"

[15:45] "Sometimes YouTube performs better than Facebook for certain offers. I don't know why. Maybe Jeremy does, but it's just how it works, right?"
[15:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Upsell Strategy = Overdeliver"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Seta ascendente, ícones de entrega de valor excepcional, cliente satisfeito
[16:00] "The best upsell strategy is to overdeliver. Again, guys, these are first principles to sales. These are things that are just boiling it down to things that we don't want to overthink, right? You want more people to accelerate and upsell, right? You got to overdeliver. That's obvious."
[16:20] "And we could go over a lot by the way if you guys have questions on upsells at the end of this presentation because there's a lot of strategies around that. I call it integrated acceleration, right?"
[16:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Optimizations = Won Deals + Lost Reasons"
NÚMEROS: Análise de wins e losses
VISUAL: Diagrama de análise de vendas, feedback loop, otimização contínua
[16:35] "The best optimizations are informed by won deals and lost reasons. I pay a lot of attention. I spend a lot of time analyzing the end of the sales funnel and what happened. I want to know exactly who bought and why. I want to know exactly who didn't buy and why."
[16:55] "Okay, most of my optimizations all the way back to the top of the funnel and marketing have to do with those things, right? Messaging, resonance, tweaks in the offer, feature benefit things like why didn't they buy and I attack that, right?"
[17:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Nobody Cares How Much You Know Until They Know How Much You Care"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Texto inspiracional, possivelmente com coração ou ícone de conexão humana
[17:20] "Lastly, nobody cares how much you know until they know how much you care. Okay, it's true. And by the way, I'll tell you guys this. The days of like unsophisticated or non-sophisticated, that's not a good word to mess up because it means not smart. But the days of reps not knowing a lot about what they do are over, right?"
[17:40] "Consumers are educated online. I see a lot of actually different closer pods or whatever it is these days. And they talk a lot about like, well, if you know sales, you don't even need to know the offer. Put them against my guys. Put them against my guys. Right?"
[17:58] "There's opportunity loss. Product knowledge is highly important and how you communicate that matters and you have to do it in a way that they feel good about."
[18:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Close = Strong Open"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Funil de vendas destacando a importância da abertura, discovery process
[18:15] "Oh, I did miss it. Thank you for that. It's very self-explanatory though. A best close is a strong open. Okay. If you didn't open well, and what I mean by that is discovery, finding the pain points, really listening, building rapport, having connectivity, and building that establishing that connection, you're going to have a hard time closing, right?"
[18:40] "So, so many people, they so many trainings I see, sales trainings, they talk so much about the closing techniques, but I'll tell you guys this, it's about the open and it's about the framing and it's about the objection blocking."
[18:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Objection Handling Hierarchy"
NÚMEROS: 3 níveis de vendedores
VISUAL: Pirâmide ou escada mostrando: Bad Salesman (evita), Good Salesman (supera), Great Salesman (antecipa)
[19:00] "If you guys are taking notes, this is a good one to write down. A bad salesman avoids objections. Right? Their thoughts, their hopes is that you don't have to write all that part, just the first part. Okay? Write this. A bad salesman avoids objections. Just that. Okay?"
[19:20] "Their thought, their hope is that maybe it will never come up and they'll just buy because of that. Whether they vocalize it or not, maybe that happens or not, but it's in their head. Believe that. A good salesman overcomes objections, right? You guys all know that though."
[19:35] "A great salesman knows the objections before their lead and they beat them to it. Okay? I tell leads why they're not going to buy or why somebody wouldn't buy way before they ever tell me that that crossed their mind and I overcome it and I rationalize it and I justify it. Right? That's what that means. The best close is a strong open."
[20:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Prerequisites to Scale"
NÚMEROS: Lista de pré-requisitos
VISUAL: Checklist ou roadmap mostrando etapas antes de escalar
[20:05] "Okay. So, here's what I call kind of prerequisites to scale. These are all things that have to do with scaling a sales operation, but they need to be addressed kind of from the beginning, right?"
[20:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Market Validation ≠ Product Market Fit"
NÚMEROS: 2 conceitos distintos
VISUAL: Diagrama de Venn ou comparação lado a lado mostrando as diferenças
[20:20] "First of all, I'm going to read through them, and there's some that I have examples and we'll talk about. Okay, first of all, market validation. Market validation is not the same as product market fit. Okay, it's not the same."
[20:35] "I ask people when I take offers, by the way, I only want to take offers when I'm scaling it with my team that have product market fit. I do consulting on things that aren't there yet. But if I'm going to scale something, it has to have product market fit already."
[20:50] "A lot of people think they have product market fit, and they don't. They have problem solution fit. Here's the difference. Okay, product market fit, and I have a slide on this I'm going to show you in a second, but product market fit solves for a lot more variables and means that your actual offer is legit and ready to scale."
[21:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Problem Solution Fit vs Product Market Fit"
NÚMEROS: Comparação entre 2 conceitos
VISUAL: Dois círculos ou caixas comparativas destacando as diferenças: Problem Solution Fit (identifica dor + quer solução) vs Product Market Fit (inclui método + preço)
[21:15] "Problem solution fit just means that you've identified a pain point and your audience wants you to solve it for them. So a lot of people get that confused because they have all these questions on why am I scaling? Why aren't I getting more sales? I know that so many people want that. There's so much demand but I can't close them."
[21:35] "It's because you have problem solution fit. You don't have product market fit which takes into account the method that you actually deliver it and the price point. We'll go over there in a second. Right. So you have to have those two things."
[21:50] "Incorporates what? I'm going to show a slide on it, but it's like the method and the mechanism that they want to deliver it at the price point. That's when you have product market fit. Okay, I'll show you in a second."
[22:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Scale Will NOT Solve Unit Economics"
NÚMEROS: Ênfase em margens unitárias
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando que escala não corrige margens ruins, setas indicando problema multiplicado
[22:10] "Scale will not solve for unit economics. Okay, won't solve for it. So what you basically are going to want to do is you're going to want to figure out what is your profit margin on an individual unit level. Okay, it's really highly important guys because if you don't have a great margin and if everything doesn't pencil on one unit scale does not solve that."
[22:35] "That's when your sales go up and your profit margin goes down. Right."
[22:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOPMD Framework"
NÚMEROS: 5 variáveis (N-O-P-M-D)
VISUAL: Framework visual mostrando: Niche, Offer, Price, Mechanism, Distribution com conexões e relações entre elementos
[22:45] "Lastly on this side NOPMD. Okay. So this is something I created right here. This whole entire chart. This right here is maybe the quickest way to explain how to build a scalable offer. It refers a lot to offer design, right? That's kind of the main concept that this is on."
[23:05] "But what's so interesting about this NOPMD, it stands for niche, offer, price, mechanism, distribution. What's really interesting about this slide that I'm going to share with you guys today is the relationship between the variables. And depending on which you cluster together, they solve for different things."
[23:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOPMD: Business Strategy vs Business Economics"
NÚMEROS: Top = Strategy, Bottom = Economics
VISUAL: Diagrama dividido horizontalmente mostrando como diferentes combinações das 5 variáveis resolvem para estratégia (topo) ou economia (base)
[23:30] "On the top, by the way, if you guys can't see it, it talks about business strategy. On the bottom of those five variables, it speaks to business economics. Strategy, economics, right? Different things."
[23:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOP = Market Validation (Problem Solution Fit)"
NÚMEROS: 3 variáveis: Niche + Offer + Price
VISUAL: Três círculos conectados ou equação visual mostrando N+O+P = Market Validation
[23:50] "Okay, we're talking about market validation and problem solution fit, right? If you look at this chart right here, that's NOP. That's niche, offer, price. Those variables together."
[24:05] "Okay, let me demonstrate. If you guys have an offer that a certain niche, a certain market segment or audience or avatar wants to buy and they are willing to pay the price for the offer that you're exchanging with nothing else involved, no other factors, you would have problem solution fit. Okay. So again, NOP is market validation, right?"
[24:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOPM = Product Market Fit"
NÚMEROS: 4 variáveis: Niche + Offer + Price + Mechanism
VISUAL: Quatro elementos conectados mostrando como o Mechanism completa o Product Market Fit
[24:35] "But check this out. It's not product market fit because that's going to solve for niche, offer, price, and mechanism. Okay, it factors in mechanism."
[24:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mechanism Example: $1,000 Training"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 price point, 6 horas de duração
VISUAL: Comparação visual: Chalkboard vs Virtual Webinar, mesmo conteúdo, mecanismos diferentes
[24:50] "Now, okay, here's the example. Niche, offer, price, mechanism. I'll give you an example, and I'm just coming up with this one right now, so it might not be super compelling, but it will make the demonstration work."
[25:05] "Let's say that I have a training that I'm going to do. It's a piece of content, right? Or it's a crash course, call it six hours, and I'm going to put you guys through a boot camp on this marketing knowledge that you guys all want to learn, right? And it's $1,000, okay?"
[25:25] "And so, you guys are the right niche. Let's say that you want to learn that and the offer is okay with you guys and all of you guys can afford $1,000. I technically have problem solution fit."
[25:38] "But let's say that the entire presentation is on a chalkboard, okay? And I tell you guys that I guarantee there's a lot of you guys here that don't want to buy it now, right?"
[25:50] "But let's say that I give this to you guys on a webinar and you guys are sitting on your couch, right? It's the same niche, same offer, same price, but now I'm delivering it virtually. I'm coming straight to you in your home. You're on your couch and you're sitting in your pajamas. It's a different mechanism, right?"
[26:10] "And now you buy because you like the mechanism that I'm delivering the content. Now we have product market fit. You're getting what you want at the price you want it in the way that you want it."
[26:25] "So it's really interesting because the mechanism makes a big difference. I mean I'll tell you guys this video content courses that's a mechanism. That's a way of delivering your value proposition."
[26:40] "So to make this extremely clear for some of you guys who might understand the concept but you're struggling to have the application of this information, it would come down to like the pillars in the offer. All of the things that your offer comes with, right?"
[26:55] "How they're receiving the information if it's an info product. Are they happy with all of the deliverables that they actually get? Because they might like the price and they might want the offer, but they're not happy with the actual mechanism that you're communicating the value through."
[27:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOPMD = Scalable Business"
NÚMEROS: 5 variáveis completas
VISUAL: Framework completo com todas as 5 variáveis conectadas, seta apontando para "Scalable Business"
[27:20] "Now, when you have niche, offer, price, mechanism, and distribution. Distribution is customer acquisition. How you're generating these clients, now you're going to have a scalable business because you've solved for all five of those variables."
[27:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "OPM = Unit Economics"
NÚMEROS: 3 variáveis: Offer + Price + Mechanism
VISUAL: Três elementos conectados mostrando como eles determinam: COGS, Fulfillment Cost, Profit Margin
[27:45] "Okay, check this out. The bottom three offer price mechanism solves for unit economics. If you have an offer and it costs a certain price based on how you deliver it is going to change your cost of goods. It's going to change your fulfillment cost and it's going to indicate your profit margin. Am I right?"
[28:05] "Right. So that's the mechanism. That's the method. So the offer, the price, and the mechanism is what you're going to want to evaluate when you're solving for unit economics."
[28:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Contribution Margin"
NÚMEROS: Profit margin sem custos fixos
VISUAL: Fórmula ou gráfico mostrando Revenue - Variable Costs = Contribution Margin
[28:25] "And you're going to have something called contribution margin. And contribution margin means what is your profit margin on a variable cost level without fixed cost. So in other words, let me say this simpler. How much money do I make from selling this one unit without the rest of all of my fixed cost? Right?"
[28:45] "So you want to have a strong contribution margin to be able to cover the rest of the business."
[28:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NOP = Willingness to Pay"
NÚMEROS: 3 primeiras variáveis
VISUAL: Três elementos conectados mostrando como determinam o preço máximo que o cliente pagará
[28:55] "The first three niche offer price represents solving for willingness to pay because you have to figure out with this niche and with this offer, how much are they willing to pay for it. Okay?"
[29:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Complete Equation = Business Profitability"
NÚMEROS: Todas as 5 variáveis + CPA
VISUAL: Framework completo mostrando como todas as variáveis juntas + Cost Per Acquisition = Business Profitability
[29:15] "And lastly, the entire equation again solves for the business profitability. The way that you did all of that and then you take into consideration cost per acquisition and all that stuff, now you can solve for business profitability."
[29:30] "Is that stuff pretty cool?"
[29:32] "Yeah."
[29:35] "Yeah, we're following. Is this different than most sales types of content?"
[29:40] "Cool, guys. It's funny. I don't even really talk about sales when I tell people how to scale a sales operation. Yeah."
[29:50] "These graphs. Is it cool to send this slide out after?"
[29:55] "Yeah. Let me get with Jeremy with the best way to do that. But yeah."
[30:00] "Okay. Thanks."
[30:02] "Yeah."
[30:05] "What are some other examples of distribution?"
[30:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Distribution Examples"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplos canais listados
VISUAL: Ícones representando: Affiliate Program, Facebook Ads, YouTube Ads, Webinars, Crash Courses, Challenges, Live Events
[30:10] "So, distribution is customer acquisition. So maybe it's an affiliate program, maybe it's Facebook ads, maybe it's YouTube ads, maybe it's a webinar model, I'm going to talk about acquisition models, maybe it's a crash course, maybe it's a challenge, right? Maybe it's a live event."
[30:30] "It's the way that you're bringing in customers. So we all know that some of them are more cost effective than others. So you don't have a scalable business until you actually have a distribution model that works for you profitably. Okay."
[30:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MSP: Minimal Sellable Product"
NÚMEROS: Conceito único de Josh
VISUAL: Comparação entre MVP (Minimal Viable Product) vs MSP (Minimal Sellable Product), destaque para o conceito original
[30:50] "Next is MSP. Okay. Who here has heard of MVP? Minimal viable product. By show of hands, most of us, right? It's a pretty popular entrepreneurial term."
[31:05] "A long time ago, I came up with something called MSP. Stands for minimal sellable product. Minimal sellable product. It's one of my favorite things. I'm telling you guys, this one thing has changed the way that I do business and I've been doing it for a long time."
[31:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MSP Definition"
NÚMEROS: Bare minimum para ir ao mercado
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando o mínimo necessário para vender vs desenvolvimento completo do produto
[31:30] "Minimal sellable product is coming up with the bare minimum that you actually have to have developed or established or ready to be able to take to market, get a response from the market and make some sales before you have to incur a bunch of other costs for something that may or may not work."
[31:50] "So, I'll give you guys an example of it. And by the way, it's okay if this is you. You don't even have to raise your hand, but I know it's either you or someone you know because this happens all the time."
[32:05] "How many of you guys, whether it's yourself or somebody that you know, you spend a lot of money developing a product, maybe it's a course or a mastermind or whatever it is, spend all this money developing it. You take it to market and it doesn't sell, right? We've done that. We know people that have done that."
[32:25] "And the reason why is because they're building the entire product. They don't have market validation and they don't have product market fit. So, what we want to do before we actually have those things and we figure out what resonates with their audience is we want to build a minimal sellable product."
[32:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MSP Strategy: Acceleration Models"
NÚMEROS: 4-8 semanas típicas
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando venda primeiro, desenvolvimento depois, semana a semana
[32:50] "Fair enough. So, here's different one of my some of my favorite ways that I like to do that. Acceleration models. Are we familiar with acceleration models? Jeremy's master internet marketing. It is a good example of an acceleration product."
[33:10] "I'm not saying he does this, by the way. That's a good example though of acceleration model. You have a bunch of information. You're delivering it over a course of time. It's usually like four to eight weeks and you're doing a training every single week, right?"
[33:28] "The cool thing about that is as long as I know the content that I'm going to go over and I come up with some pitch design and I know how I'm going to sell it, I can take that to market and start selling it with nothing because let's say it's an eight-week acceleration model. I have eight weeks after point of sale to actually build out that product, right?"
[33:50] "I could build out one per week."
[33:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real Example: Grant Cardone Cold Calling Course"
NÚMEROS: Centenas de milhares de dólares vendidos, 6 meses antes de desenvolver
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando vendas massivas antes do desenvolvimento do produto
[34:00] "So when I worked for Grant back in the day, he had this course that he called like something about cold calling, like master cold calling, and we sold the shit out of it. Like we sold I want to say at least a couple hundred thousand dollars worth of the program and he didn't even develop it until like six months later and everybody was always freaking out."
[34:20] "His staff were always the ones that freak out. They were like, 'Yeah, dude, like we got to get this shit done. Like we've been selling it to people and like Grant didn't give a fuck.' Like you guys don't understand like it doesn't matter. It's like we're developing. But they knew that when they bought."
[34:40] "That's exactly right. And I like how he said that at the end too. They knew that when they bought it. Sometimes you could be really transparent around that. But a lot of the times you don't have to because you have eight weeks to build one training per week, right?"
[35:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MSP Benefits"
NÚMEROS: Comparação de riscos financeiros
VISUAL: Dois cenários: Refund pequeno vs Investimento grande desperdiçado, setas mostrando otimização em tempo real
[35:05] "Here's why that's powerful, though. If it fails, you can refund some people, right? Instead, it's way better. You refund five grand or whatever it is because the offer failed, we're assuming. So, it's not going to be a lot of sales rather than you spend a ton of time, energy, and money developing this whole thing and then it failing, right?"
[35:25] "So, I've done that before. I've just refunded people. I'm like, 'Ah, this offer sucked.' And then I fixed it, right?"
[35:35] "But what's even cooler that's more common that happens is it's not converting and so you start tweaking and optimizing it and adjusting the pitch and adjusting the features and adjusting the content, right? You start changing that in kind of real time and your marketing starts improving and then you nail it and you didn't have to redo anything because nothing was built yet. You're just adjusting the offer, right?"
[36:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "MSP Examples"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplas opções listadas
VISUAL: Lista com ícones: Weekly Pod Coaching, One-on-One Coaching, Waiting Lists, Delayed/Dripped Fulfillment
[36:05] "Weekly pod coaching or one-on-one coaching, waiting lists, right? Pretty much anything with a delayed or dripped fulfillment will give you a lot of time to be able to build it after the fact."
[36:20] "And so the idea is you go to market with your pitch design, right? You adjust the offer design based on the resonance or the feedback that you get and then you invest resources for product development after you've been able to generate a lot of money."
[36:40] "Okay, does that make sense? And by the way, guys, do you understand why this is so important? It's not like I'm over here like, I need to make some money right now. I'm just gonna sell something and I'll figure it out. That's actually different because people do that, right? That's different."
[36:58] "This is strategic because I know I'm not going to go spend a ton of money. And by the way, this is very similar to MVP. I'll tell you this. I know this guy from Silicon Valley. He's made a lot of money building softwares and apps and all this stuff. If you see the first version of it, it looks like shit. It looks like a little website and he's telling me it's some big app, right?"
[37:20] "But it's because he goes to market with it. He adjusts the offer. He figures out what his audience actually wants and then he starts to build it all out. Okay, I'm going to speed this up a little here."
[37:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Financial Modeling & Capacity Mapping"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: 76 applications projetadas vs 72 reais
VISUAL: Spreadsheet ou dashboard mostrando projeções vs resultados reais, alta precisão
[37:40] "Next is financial modeling and capacity mapping. Okay, so here's all I'm going to say. You have to do financial modeling to scale a sales operation. I don't understand this. It's I'll say that this is the most confusing thing me and Jeremy work with. We talk to new clients all the time."
[38:00] "We're like, 'So, what do you guys want to spend?' They're like, 'I don't know. 250. How's that sound?' I'm like, 'I don't know.' Like, 'Well, what's your cost per acquisition? You know, what's your profit margin? What's this? What's that?' We ask all these questions. They have no idea. And it's just weird to me. It's so weird to me."
[38:20] "You have to do I have financial models and spreadsheets for every funnel that you guys can think of. Me and Jeremy plug them in all day long, right? And so, I know. And guess what? Here's what's really cool. I projected this one challenge this week. I projected that we would have 76 applications on day three of this event. And we had 72. Right?"

[39:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reps Are The #1 Thing"

But when I do that, what it also shows you is the amount of reps. The number one thing that sales and sales operations is what, Sean?"
[39:25] "Reps."
[39:27] "How many reps did you just have?"
[39:30] "We had four."

[39:35] "We have 30."
[39:37] "In what period of time?"
[39:40] "Like less than two weeks."
[39:42] "In two weeks. And a lot of people are going to struggle finding those reps. So, here's the thing. Based on if you guys say, so I don't even talk in terms of revenue anymore, guys. I talk in terms of the stuff that matters."
[40:00] "I don't tell clients, hey, we need to be at $5 million this month. I tell people, hey, we need 60 reps this month because that's the actionable thing that matters, right? The revenue is going to come based on having all these other things in place, right?"
[40:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Operational Constraints Beyond Sales"
NÚMEROS: Foco em fulfillment e bottlenecks
VISUAL: Diagrama de processo mostrando múltiplos pontos de gargalo além de vendas
[40:25] "So, you want to know what your capacity is, namely your rep capacity. Then I look at all the other operational lags and bottlenecks to also scale. If you have large fulfillment, Mike, you guys are crushing it at wealth accelerators. You don't have a problem with sales, right?"
[40:45] "If you could sell twice as many trucks right now, don't you feel confident that you'd be able to sell it?"
[40:52] "Dude, we could sell 100, but the hard part is just the fulfillment."
[40:58] "Okay. And he has a great offer. So, this is not the greatest example because he has a strong offer, but there's a lot of fulfillment. But the point is this. You want to look at all the other things, the variable fulfillment and cogs that once you sell it, what are the bottlenecks?"
[41:15] "What's going to be the hardest things to fix and put strategies into place to overcome those? Because I'm telling you guys, you can't scale a sales operation by making more deals. What are all the other things that are going to be the constraints as you start to do more volume?"
[41:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reduce Constraints"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: $2M/mês com one-on-one coaching = 5,000 horas
VISUAL: Cálculo visual mostrando impossibilidade matemática, necessidade de ajuste do modelo
[41:40] "Okay, reduce the amount of constraints. When you draw up your offer and you're looking at the unit economics and everything that comes up with your offer, look at all the things that you need more of."
[41:55] "If you have one-on-one coaching and your goal is $2 million a month in revenue. How many hours of one-on-one coaching is that? You know what's funny? Sometimes they're like, 'Yeah, I'm going to be at 2 million a month. I'm doing one-on-one coaching.' I'm like, 'So, you're going to do 5,000 hours of coaching this week?'"
[42:15] "You know, like a lot of the times you'll find stuff like that. And so, you have to adjust your offer. Okay, maybe I can't do one-on-one coaching. I have to do pod coaching. And then you go back to unit economics and it's more profitable."
[42:30] "Anyways, my point is you have to limit the constraints."
[42:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "OTE Modeling (On-Track Earnings)"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: $50,000 potencial por rep
VISUAL: Tabela mostrando quotas, comissões e earnings potenciais para sales reps
[42:40] "Lastly, OTE modeling. Does anybody know what OTE is?"
[42:45] "On track earnings."
[42:48] "Okay, earning potential for the sales rep. So, basically, it's saying if we forecast out and our sales team hits their metrics and their quotas, this is how much they're expected to make."
[43:05] "The reason that's so important is because if you don't model out your on-track earnings when you're forecasting all this stuff, you might say that you want to be at X amount of dollars per month and you have a model that means that your sales reps would make $50,000 and you're expecting to have great sales reps to sell it, right?"
[43:25] "Well, now it doesn't work because no sales reps are going to work for you. So, it's really important you consider that and pay your reps well. Okay."
[43:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Main Constraints Are NOT Sales"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Diagrama mostrando múltiplos fatores além de vendas: fulfillment, operations, systems, etc.
[43:40] "Like I said, usually the main things that actually prevent sales operations from scaling are not actually sales or all the other constraints. Okay, I don't have that much left, guys. I'll go through this quickly."
[43:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Architecture and Engineering"
NÚMEROS: Seção principal
VISUAL: Título de seção com elementos de design técnico/arquitetural
[44:00] "Sales architecture and engineering. Okay, so I have something called the pitch design canvas."
[44:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pitch Design Canvas"
NÚMEROS: Framework para design de pitch
VISUAL: Canvas ou framework visual mostrando elementos de um pitch efetivo
[44:10] "Pitch design is everything. Pitch design, by the way, if I just spent my time on sales calls, my value is going to be limited to the amount of calls that I can take, right? That's it. And then I'm maxed out."
[44:25] "However, pitch design is how I sell through every other rep because I'm designing the entire pitch and training on exactly like the ingenuity, the innovativeness of how we're selling this and then I train all the reps on it."
[44:40] "And pitch design, by the way, is how you differentiate, is how you increase your prices. It's how you do all of those things. Client facing, right? And you're really illustrating how good your offer design is."
[44:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pitch Design Variables"
NÚMEROS: 6 variáveis principais
VISUAL: Lista ou diagrama mostrando: Existence Narrative, Current vs Desired State, 3 P's of Pain, Elevator Pitch, Unique Enforcement Frames, Main Pillars vs Details
[45:00] "So these are just a few variables that I solve for in my pitch design. So if you have a pitch and you want to make it a little bit better, these are just a few things that you're going to want to work on."
[45:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Existence Narrative"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Timeline ou storytelling visual mostrando origem do produto/oferta
[45:20] "The first is existence narrative. A lot of people don't in their sales pitch share why you created the product or the offer. Like how did that happen? It's so compelling, right? It's especially because a lot of people in this space, guys, we all know it. They think that you just want to get rich. You want to make a lot of money."
[45:40] "And while for most of us that is also a goal, right? There's more to it. And so you want to illustrate how you got to this point. And while you share your story, it's blocking a lot of objections, right?"
[45:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Current State vs Desired State"
NÚMEROS: 2 estados comparados
VISUAL: Duas caixas ou pontos conectados por ponte/seta, representando jornada do cliente
[46:00] "Second thing, current state versus desired state. That's the entire point of your pitch, it needs to clearly demonstrate how you're getting them from the current state to the desired state."
[46:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "3 P's of Pain"
NÚMEROS: 3 elementos: Pain, Problem, Pressure
VISUAL: Três círculos ou camadas mostrando progressão: Pain → Problem → Pressure (urgência)
[46:20] "I have something called three Ps of pain. Starts with pain. You have to identify the pain. You have to identify the problem that the pain's causing. And then you have to identify the pressure that's creating urgency for them wanting to solve it."
[46:38] "If you can't identify those three Ps early on in the sales call, chances are you're probably not going to get the deal because they don't have enough pain to actually want the solution, right? So, I spend a lot of my time on that."
[46:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Elevator Pitch"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Exemplo de pitch conciso e impactante, possivelmente com antes/depois
[47:00] "Elevator pitch is obvious. You have to have a way to explain your offer in a really sexy way. I mean, for Mike, by the way, just so you guys know, we ended up calling it an alternative investment opportunity, and we came up with all these different ways. And dude, was it not freaking awesome?"
[47:20] "It smashed. I mean, you have to figure out like what is the sexiest way that I could explain this and don't focus on it being super gimmicky, but you can't just articulate what it is. You have to in your elevator pitch say why it's the best thing that they've ever seen, unlike anything else they've ever had. Right?"
[47:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Unique Enforcement Frames"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplos frames/conceitos
VISUAL: Exemplos de frames únicos que justificam superioridade da oferta
[47:45] "Unique enforcement frames are what I call different little bits, different things that you could explain that really justify why your offer is the best. So, this one's kind of hard to articulate and I wish that I had an actual example in this moment, but basically I take everybody through a closing deck."
[48:05] "It's a slide deck when I actually extract money when I'm on a closing call and I have visualizations or concepts or stories that help me land certain points other than just simply reading through the details that the product has. Right?"
[48:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sell Main Pillars, NOT Details"
NÚMEROS: Exemplo: $30,000 mastermind, 100% collections, zero planos de pagamento
VISUAL: Comparação visual: Pilares principais (grandes) vs Detalhes (pequenos), ênfase nos pilares
[48:30] "Lastly, sell the main pillars, not details. I'll tell you guys an interesting fact. It's just an interesting fact. We have a new client, okay? Sold hundreds of thousands of dollars in the last couple weeks on this new mastermind. Okay? So, it's a $30,000 mastermind."
[48:50] "So, we got a good amount of buyers on it so far in the first couple weeks here. Not a single person asked me the dates or locations of the mastermind. I just found that so fascinating. I'm like, not a single one of these people even asked me where or when it is. And they bought and they wired 30K and we had 100% collections, by the way. Not a single payment plan."
[49:15] "So, that really is a good example of sell the main value pillars, not the details. I don't even get into the details because a lot of people what they do is they talk themselves out of a deal because they think that they know every single little intricate detail that the lead wants to see and then you say something that they're like, 'Oh, that's my cousin's bar mitzvah that weekend. I'm not going.'"
[49:40] "But if doesn't come up and then they buy and now all of a sudden they're down the road and they're going to go to the mastermind, they're going to figure it out at that point, right? Point is, it's just interesting how a lot of people don't care about the details. So, sell the sizzle."
[50:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Funnels & Buying Gradient"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplos tipos de funnels
VISUAL: Lista de funnels: Mini Webinar, VSSL, Regular Webinar, Crash Course, Master Class, Boot Camp - todos = Content Funnels
[50:05] "Okay. Buying gradient. Here's the topic that I'm going to end on. This is content funnels, right? A lot of people, and by the way, this isn't a slash to anybody that has name for these funnels. It's just a way that I communicate them better because certain people refer to certain funnels with different names and then it gets confusing and semantics."
[50:25] "So like Jeremy, he calls them mini webinar and mini webinar 2.0. And then there's like VSSLs and regular webinars and all these other things and crash courses and master classes and boot camps. I call them content funnels because it's the same shit you're just doing content in different styles and different lengths of time."
[50:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Buying Gradient"
NÚMEROS: Progressão de awareness
VISUAL: Escada ou gradiente ascendente mostrando níveis: Cold Lead → Problem Aware → Solution Aware → Brand Aware → Product/Service Aware → Hot Lead
[50:50] "The important thing to understand about content funnels, regardless of what you're putting people through to sell your stuff, it's all about the buying gradient. We call it a buying gradient. You have to take people up the gradient to the point where there's enough value, trust, and credibility where they're willing to pay for what you have, right? It's called the buying gradient."
[51:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Heat Index"
NÚMEROS: Progressão de temperatura do lead
VISUAL: Termômetro ou escala de cores mostrando: Cold → Warm → Hot, com níveis de awareness
[51:20] "And so, here's something we call the heat index, right? From the top of the funnel. They're not that hot of a lead. They go through everything you have in all of your content. They're problem aware. They're solution aware. They're brand aware. They're product and service aware."
[51:38] "Now, they're the most aware of your solution and everything that you have to solve their problem. And you took them through this valuable gradient with the time that they spent with you and now they're ready to buy and they're a hot lead."
[51:55] "A lot of the times I'll tell you guys this. If you're saying, 'How do I have a cold lead and how do I make them a warmer lead before my sales team talks to them?' It's content. That's literally it. Have them watch more content. They'll be a hotter lead by the time you talk to them."
[52:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Every Piece of Content = Closer or Further"
NÚMEROS: Nenhum número específico
VISUAL: Setas bidirecionais mostrando que conteúdo pode aproximar ou afastar do negócio
[52:20] "So, the way that we look at content is every piece of content is getting your audience closer or further away from doing business with you. So, it obviously depends on what type of content it is and how it's designed and what it's supposed to do."
[52:38] "So, when we talk about indoctrination sequence, he's talking about the Hydra, if I'm remembering that name right, the Hydra strategy. And he's saying he's slamming them with three emails a day. Jeremy's smart and he knows a lot of this stuff and we work together on a lot of this stuff."
[52:55] "And I'm willing to bet you guys that in those emails there's lots of content. It's not, 'Hey, are you ready? Hey, are you ready? Do you want to jump on a phone call? Hey, did you get my last email? Hey, did you get my last three emails?' That just annoys people. You have to get them content."
[53:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Capital Raising Example"
NÚMEROS: $250K-$500K wires, 90% mesmas perguntas

[53:20] "And so, I'll tell you guys this because it's interesting and it speaks directly to this point. When I was raising capital online from people that I never met before and they're wiring me $250,000 to $500,000 at a time. Obviously, lower amounts, but those were the bigger ones that I would get between a quarter million to 500 grand. Never spoke to them before. Right."
[53:45] "What was so interesting about this is that 90% of the questions that I would get on this call, and again, Jeremy ran the ads for this, so he's familiar with all this stuff, guys. We would do a lot of this stuff together. Here's what's super interesting. 90% of every question that I would get on every call was the exact same."
[54:05] "So, I decided, this is bullshit. I'm not answering these questions anymore. So, then in my indoctrination sequence, I told them, I don't talk to people that haven't watched these videos. They jump on and I'm polite about it, right? I'm just being blunt with you guys, but that's what I say."
[54:25] "They jump on the phone. Have you watched these videos? No. Let's reschedule. That's what I do. Okay. So, what ended up happening is people get on the phone. This is a good one for you, Sean. We'll talk about it later."

[57:25] "So, naturally, if you have a long sales page, you're going to have to have content that is doing all those things that's taking them up the buying gradient. It is the exact same with your different content funnels."

[58:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real Challenge Example"
NÚMEROS: $1,000 oferta → $25,000 oferta, mudança de conteúdo dia 3
VISUAL: Comparação de dois challenges: mesmo formato, conteúdo diferente, resultados drasticamente diferentes
[58:05] "So, I'll give you guys one example, and I won't say any names, but Jeremy and I work on an account, and they had a challenge model. And what they initially did is they had a challenge model where they ended up selling a $1,000 offer at the end of the week. And it smashed it. They did that a bunch of times."
[58:25] "Then they said, 'Josh, we need you because we want to sell a $25,000 offer.' I was like, 'Perfect.' First question, 'How do you guys feel about the content of the challenge?' We got it. It's dialed in. It smashes. Trust me, we feel so good about it. Cool."
[58:45] "We get on the challenge model and I had a heart attack day one. It was the most basic fluffy type of content that you guys could see, right? With respect to what they can offer. They're talented, sophisticated individuals that I respect a lot, but compared to what they could have shared, it was very fluffy. It was very watered down."
[59:10] "This was going to bomb, guys, right? It was going to bomb. You know it was going to bomb because that content was enough to get the weight of a $1,000 decision. But this would not take them up the buying gradient to be able to get them to pay $25,000 a few days later. Okay."
[59:30] "So, what do we do? Emergency. Change your entire content. What were you guys thinking? These are the conversations we have. And they did."

[01:00:55] "I actually saw a couple confused looks. So, let me break that down for a second. What happens is, so we always say as sales speakers, you have to speak to the A's in the room."

[01:01:50] "You want to cater to the A's. You want to speak to the A's, the main perfect fit. And your content has to do that. So, it was just interesting in real time that we change all the content. All of a sudden, the A's start coming on."

[01:04:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sales Closing Deck & Pipe Cleaner"
NÚMEROS: Frequência sugerida: 2x por mês
VISUAL: Conceito de pipe cleaner event, Zoom call com múltiplos prospects
[01:04:25] "And then, sales, a closing deck and a pipe cleaner. A pipe cleaner just means, guys, if so, here's a really good way to increase sales. I do it all the time to organizations when I come in."
[01:04:40] "If you guys want to increase sales and you have a bunch of leads in the pipeline, I call it a pipe cleaner. Based on the time that you could commit to whatever you're doing. Either it's once a month or it's every week if you could or it's twice a month."
[01:05:00] "I have a pipe cleaner event where I just have a Zoom or a webinar or whatever it is. I prefer Zoom where it's more intimate. You invite everybody that's currently in your pipeline that your sales reps are working onto a call. You do a customized training specifically for those leads or you just do an open Q&A. It's a pipe cleaner."
[01:05:25] "You invite everybody on. You give them more content and your sales reps invite them to it and then they try to close them afterwards."
[01:05:35] "When I'm actually on the sales call or when I'm actually get on one of these pipe cleaner calls, I say, 'Hey, by the way guys, I know a lot of you guys are right there on the fence. You're saying, Hey Josh, it looks like you guys have a ton of value, but we're just not sure.' And that's a fair thing to say."
[01:05:55] "So, let's make sure that you guys find that certainty and today I hope we're able to do that. And then I go into whatever it is. So, it's specific to whatever we're going over or to wherever they're at. That's a pipe cleaner event."
[01:06:15] "What do you guys think about that one by the way?"
[01:06:18] "It's solid, right? Because it's so easy. Everybody could do that today, but barely anybody does it. So, just have a pipe cleaner event. Okay."

Your reps need to pretend that they're your leads and you send them off onto the internet as if they were looking for you but didn't know who you are."
[01:10:25] "What are the things they find? What comes up? What are the other offers, right? What are the questions that you have when you're looking? [01:11:00] "Think about how powerful Facebook is. It's the reason we all use it. [01:15:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Integrity-Based Extraction"

I don't So, the thing that I say is I'm never going to do anything that's going to take the cheese off someone's macaroni. I'm never going to do anything that's going to shut off your hot water, right?"
[01:16:10] "So, the point is not to take advantage and put people in the worst financial situation. But, dude, you guys got to know how to find money with people that are willing to pay it if they knew how to find it. And then we just tell them how to do it."
[01:16:30] "And that's how our average sales price and our overall close rate goes through the roof. Okay, so with the time that I had here today, this is what I prepared for you guys. I hope you guys got a lot of value out of it. But yeah, I'm happy to do any questions if we had any time for it."

[01:17:35] "So if you're to your point, an automation, what'd you say, an investment firm?"
[01:17:42] "Like a automated YouTube channel."
[01:17:45] "Yeah. I would screen share and show. By the way guys, this is what it looks like. This is like some of our client results. So this is how it works. I'd break it down for them and spend some time. Then I'd say, 'Hey, here's some case studies. This is what we've done for some of our clients.'"
[01:18:05] "I would show things that they actually want to see that are going to build trust in them. Yeah. Not another webinar. It's not meant to be like emotional and state of mind and all this other stuff. Like you want to show them the content that's going to get them from not ready to buy to ready to buy."
[01:18:25] "And in that situation, I would show them the things that they want to see. And it's going to be results. It's going to be how it works. You have to remember this. A lot of people don't invest in things they don't know about, right? If it's confusing to them, a lot of them don't do it. So, break it down. Show them how it works."
[01:18:50] "On that note, for the pipe cleaner events, are those closed loss and follow-ups or like what specific what's"
[01:19:00] "I don't do it from close loss because I find that that's not the most leveraged use of our time. We put them into other automation stuff and things like that. Find other ways to re-engage them. But these are leads that are currently in the sales cycle."
[01:19:20] "So, they're being worked. They have not been marked lost yet. They may have ghosted."
[01:19:28] "Yeah."
[01:19:30] "And you haven't heard from them for a few days, but for whatever reason, they're not closed out. They're still there."
[01:19:38] "Nurture follow."
[01:19:40] "Yeah."

[01:19:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pipe Cleaner Frequency"
NÚMEROS: 2x por mês recomendado, 20-50 pessoas ideal
VISUAL: Calendário mostrando cadência bi-semanal, tamanho ideal de grupo
[01:19:50] "So, it's going to depend on the amount of people because you want it to be high leveraged. If you have one lead, I'm not going to do that often. But if you're scaling up and you have 20 to 50 people and your offer is $100,000 or even $15,000 or $10,000 and you can get that many people on, I would do it every week as often as you can commit to it."
[01:20:15] "But to answer your question, I typically have clients do this twice a month. Every other week is a cadence that makes sense. And I don't advertise it at all. It's a private invite from the sales rep that's working with them."
[01:20:35] "If people know that you do it all the time, that's where your show rate gets destroyed. It's like, 'Hey dude, by the way, I was talking to my manager about some of the things that you were saying and like they're valid and we want to answer them.'"
[01:20:50] "So, we were actually thinking about doing a special training for other people that were kind of in the same position as you. Is that something that you'd want to come to? I think it could answer a lot of your questions and you invite them."
[01:21:08] "So, yeah, once or twice a month there's someone here. Yeah."
[01:21:15] "Yeah. For the existence narrative. Is that generally philanthropic or is that can be any premise that makes sense or"
[01:21:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Existence Narrative Example"
NÚMEROS: Milhares de clientes prévios mencionados
VISUAL: Timeline mostrando: Comunidade existente → Demanda do mercado → Decisão de expandir oferta
[01:21:30] "I mean people want to know that you're good people? I don't lead with that. I also find it's kind of patronizing. Like I don't tell them like you know right before I started this company I went on a mission trip to Mexico and the look on these kids' faces made me decide I need to start a new offer to donate more. Like I think those I don't like when people do that personally. It doesn't feel right. So I don't do that."
[01:21:55] "But here's an I'll give you an example of a real one that I use right now. I have a client and they already had a very successful community, very successful. Almost all of you guys in this room would know it and I'm just not going to mention it because I don't know if I can."
[01:22:15] "But point is they had thousands of people that have already purchased one of their offers and it was basically around well I won't even say it did a certain thing and what happened is they got really good at that. I'm just answering a question real quick."
[01:22:35] "They basically were successful there but they had all these thousands of people that started reaching out and saying dude this was so great but I wish we had the next step like yes this worked but now all of a sudden I'm successful in this part of my business and we're experiencing problems in other places."
[01:22:55] "And then we said now at the same time we were already building all these other things for our portfolio CEOs because we want them to know how to run their businesses more efficiently so on one hand we had a community with people begging us to do this and on the other hand we were already doing it internally."
[01:23:15] "So, we just made the decision that we weren't going to be selfish. All of these proprietary methodologies that we built to take advantage ourselves. We decided that we're going to go ahead and offer it to the public."
[01:23:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Existence Narrative Power"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplos frames em uma história
VISUAL: Breakdown mostrando: Demanda (milhares querendo), Credibilidade (já pagaram), Propriedade (metodologias únicas), Decisão (não ser egoísta)
[01:23:40] "And so, I take them through all this whole timeline that shows them that and they're like, 'Oh shit.' So, dude, there's so much framing in there. I showed them how many people want it. I covered how credible we are. I covered how many other people, thousands of people have already given us money."
[01:24:00] "I covered how proprietary it is that we already were doing it for ourselves. So, you see how there's so much value in just explaining why we created it."
[01:24:15] "That's an existence narrative. Cool. I'll talk Yeah, I'm here all weekend, so I'll talk to you guys more. But, uh, yeah, Jeremy, I'm done. It's all you."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
700+ slides de conteúdo de treinamento
7 métricas principais de sales ops
5 variáveis do framework NOPMD (Niche, Offer, Price, Mechanism, Distribution)
4-8 semanas típicas para acceleration models
76 aplicações projetadas vs 72 reais (95% de precisão)
4 reps → 30 reps em 2 semanas (crescimento de 650%)
$1,000 → $25,000 mudança de oferta
$30,000 mastermind com 100% de coleta, zero planos de pagamento
$250K-$500K wires de cold traffic
90% das perguntas eram as mesmas em calls
45 minutos de vídeo de indoctrinação
2 D leads fecharam $25K cada
$800K exemplo de capital oculto no mercado de ações
600 credit score mínimo para financiamento
0% APR por 12 meses estratégia de crédito
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Framework NOPMD:
NOP (Niche + Offer + Price) = Market Validation / Problem Solution Fit
NOPM (+ Mechanism) = Product Market Fit
OPM (Offer + Price + Mechanism) = Unit Economics
NOP (primeiros 3) = Willingness to Pay
NOPMD (completo) = Scalable Business
MSP (Minimal Sellable Product):
Vender antes de construir completamente
Acceleration models de 4-8 semanas
Construir uma semana por vez após venda
Ajustar oferta em tempo real baseado em feedback
Refund pequeno vs investimento desperdiçado
Objection Hierarchy:
Bad Salesman: evita objeções
Good Salesman: supera objeções
Great Salesman: antecipa e bloqueia objeções
Buying Gradient & Heat Index:
Cold → Problem Aware → Solution Aware → Brand Aware → Product/Service Aware → Hot
Conteúdo = ferramenta para subir o gradiente
Quantidade/profundidade de conteúdo deve balancear com peso da decisão
Capital & Credit Extraction:
Perguntas sobre 401k, mercado de ações, advisors financeiros
Identificar capital "escondido"
Estratégia de 0% credit card por 12 meses
Nunca "tirar cheese do macaroni" ou "desligar água quente"
Pipe Cleaner Events:
Frequência: 2x por mês
20-50 pessoas ideal
Convite privado do rep
Conteúdo customizado para leads em nurture
Screen share, case studies, how it works
ESTRUTURA:
Prerequisites to Scale:
Market Validation (≠ Product Market Fit)
Unit Economics (scale não resolve)
Financial Modeling & Capacity Mapping
OTE Modeling (On-Track Earnings)
Constraint Reduction
Pitch Design Canvas - 6 Variáveis:
Existence Narrative (por que criou)
Current State vs Desired State
3 P's of Pain (Pain → Problem → Pressure)
Elevator Pitch (sexy explanation)
Unique Enforcement Frames
Sell Main Pillars, NOT Details
4 Types of Rep Training:
Sales Training
Process Training (CRM, systems)
Product Knowledge (completar programa inteiro)
POV Training (perspectiva do cliente, pesquisa competitiva)
Lead Grading System:
A leads: financeiramente qualificados, alta intenção
B leads: qualificados mas menor urgência
C leads: baixa qualificação financeira
D leads: sem capacidade financeira aparente
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Contribution Margin = Profit sem custos fixos
Unit Economics deve funcionar antes de escalar
Financial modeling permite projetar com 95%+ precisão
Foco em número de reps, não revenue
OTE modeling previne problemas de retenção de reps
Market comparables determinam price sensitivity
Elasticity: maximizar preço onde frequência de buyers ainda gera mais profit
Content depth deve balancear com decision weight
DURAÇÃO: ~1h 24min FORMATO: Apresentação de mastermind com Q&A
